reviewSummary: All four specialist reviews conclude that the scoped password-reset demonstration satisfies AC1–AC8 and that its final evidence is coherently bound to commit a5d1093d7e1b65bd8d874b16ce832ba0840d6452 with a clean scoped state and passing post-commit test. The implementation demonstrates expiring single-use tokens, neutral and deferred request handling, identifier-free telemetry, deterministic single-process concurrency and retry behavior, and dependency-failure isolation. Readiness applies only to the declared synthetic, single-process scope; durable audit delivery, persistent cross-process atomicity, production scheduling, and deployment behavior were not established.
narrowClaim: The covered evidence has not changed since it was sealed by the holder of the corresponding signing key.

residualFindings.title: Audit delivery is intentionally best-effort rather than durably guaranteed
Atomicity and scheduling guarantees are scoped to one process and injected test boundaries
Audit invocation is response-safe but not durably persisted
Atomicity and scheduling guarantees do not extend beyond one process

limitations: Assessment is limited to the supplied evidence digest; no commands were independently executed and no files outside the embedded final artifacts were inspected.

The in-memory token store is atomic only within one JavaScript process and does not survive restart. Multi-process or production use requires a persistent shared store with transactional or compare-and-set semantics.
The injected scheduler is a deterministic demonstration boundary, not a durable production queue with retries, monitoring, or delivery guarantees.
Audit submission is best-effort and non-blocking; the implementation guarantees safe invocation and neutral response behavior, not durable audit persistence.
Repository-wide canonical validation, hosted verification, deployment proof, release checks, and human acceptance were explicitly outside the supplied task scope and remain unavailable.

The token store is intentionally in-memory and its reservation transition is atomic only within one JavaScript process. It does not survive restarts or coordinate multiple workers; production use requires a persistent shared store with transactional or compare-and-set semantics.

Token entropy and unpredictability depend entirely on the injected generateToken implementation. The demonstration uses synthetic deterministic values and does not establish production cryptographic token generation.
The scheduler contract is injected and demonstrated with an in-memory deferred scheduler. Durability, bounded retries, monitoring, and delivery guarantees are outside the supplied scope.
Successful audit persistence is not guaranteed because dispatch is best-effort; the evidence establishes safe invocation and response isolation, not durable audit receipt.

Rate limiting, abuse prevention, user-session invalidation, authorization around the final password change, transport security, data retention, dependency provenance, hosted behavior, and deployment controls were not demonstrated and are outside the stated acceptance criteria.
Only the supplied artifacts and recorded tests were reviewed; repository-wide canonical validation, hosted verification, release checks, and human acceptance remain unavailable.
The evidence demonstrates only the supplied single-process synthetic workspace; no hosted, browser, deployment, or repository-wide validation was supplied or required.

The in-memory token store does not test cross-process atomicity, persistence across restart, or integration with a durable queue/shared transactional store. These are explicitly outside the demonstration boundary.
Audit tests establish safe invocation and response-path isolation, not guaranteed durable audit persistence when the transport stalls or fails.

No external email provider, datastore, queue, or end-to-end HTTP integration tests are present; dependency-injected unit tests are adequate for the declared narrow scope but do not establish production integration behavior.

The post-commit test evidence reports success and exit code 0 but does not include individual test output or an exact post-commit pass count; the earlier pre-commit validation reports 16 passed and 0 failed.
Repository-wide canonical validation, hosted verification, deployment proof, release checks, and human acceptance were explicitly unavailable and are outside the supplied task scope.
No independent execution was performed during this review; conclusions rely solely on the supplied evidence digest and its final-state binding.
The evidence proves deterministic behavior for the synthetic test environment, not real-world timing indistinguishability under production schedulers, load, or network conditions.
No commands were independently executed; this synthesis relies solely on the supplied evidence digest.
The post-commit test record reports success and exit code 0 but does not include individual test output or an exact pass count; the earlier validation reports 16 passing tests.
The evidence covers a synthetic dependency-injected workspace, not hosted behavior, end-to-end HTTP integration, external email, datastore, or queue integrations.

Production concerns including cryptographic token generation, rate limiting, abuse prevention, session invalidation, transport security, authorization around password changes, deployment controls, and dependency provenance were outside scope.
Repository-wide canonical validation, release checks, deployment proof, and human acceptance were not supplied.
The evidence does not prove real-world timing indistinguishability under production load, network variance, or scheduler behavior.
This synthesis is model judgement over supplied evidence, not an independent certification or security guarantee.
